arguments.name: explore-aot-issues
arguments.prompt:
Explore the .NET project in /tmp/sv-d57cc78fdb1c493495c4ad025e6cb367/before/ to identify AOT (Ahead-of-Time compilation) compatibility issues. This is an Azure.ResourceManager SDK project.

Look at all the key files and patterns that would typically cause AOT issues:

1. Look at the .csproj file for current AOT settings
2. Search for reflection usage patterns: `typeof`, `Type.GetType`, `Activator.CreateInstance`, `MakeGenericType`, `GetMethod`, `GetProperty`, `Invoke(`, `Assembly.Load`
3. Search for JSON serialization patterns - look for any `JsonSerializer`, `JsonSerializerOptions`, `JsonConverter`, custom converters
4. Look for `IUtf8JsonSerializable` and serialization infrastructure
5. Search for `dynamic` keyword usage
6. Look at the Shared/ directory for serialization infrastructure 
7. Search for `[UnconditionalSuppressMessage]`, `[DynamicallyAccessedMembers]`, `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]` - existing AOT annotations
8. Look at GenericOperationSource.cs since it likely uses reflection
9. Look at ArmOperation*.cs files
10. Look at the Extensions/ directory
11. Look for any converter classes (files matching *Converter*)
12. Search for `Expression.` and `Delegate` creation patterns
13. Look at the Common/ directory structure and key model files
14. Look at Resources/ directory structure
15. Look at ManagementGroup/ directory

Report ALL findings with file paths and relevant code snippets. Be thorough - list every file and line that would need attention for AOT compatibility.

data.reasoningText:
This is a large .NET project with 456 CS files. Making it AOT-compatible involves several common patterns:

1. Adding `<IsAotCompatible>true</IsAotCompatible>` to the csproj
2. Replacing reflection-based JSON serialization with source generators
3. Adding `[JsonSerializable]` attributes
4. Using `[DynamicallyAccessedMembers]` annotations where reflection is used
5. Replacing `Type.GetType()`, `Activator.CreateInstance()` calls
6. Adding trim/AOT analyzer warnings suppression or fixing them

Let me first try to build the project to see the current state, then analyze common AOT issues.


Before diving into a full build, I should examine the project structure and identify which files are most likely to have AOT compatibility issues.

arguments.name: explore-aot-issues
arguments.prompt:
Explore the .NET project in /tmp/sv-d57cc78fdb1c493495c4ad025e6cb367/before/ to identify AOT (Ahead-of-Time compilation) compatibility issues. This is an Azure.ResourceManager SDK project.

Look at all the key files and patterns that would typically cause AOT issues:

1. Look at the .csproj file for current AOT settings
2. Search for reflection usage patterns: `typeof`, `Type.GetType`, `Activator.CreateInstance`, `MakeGenericType`, `GetMethod`, `GetProperty`, `Invoke(`, `Assembly.Load`
3. Search for JSON serialization patterns - look for any `JsonSerializer`, `JsonSerializerOptions`, `JsonConverter`, custom converters
4. Look for `IUtf8JsonSerializable` and serialization infrastructure
5. Search for `dynamic` keyword usage
6. Look at the Shared/ directory for serialization infrastructure 
7. Search for `[UnconditionalSuppressMessage]`, `[DynamicallyAccessedMembers]`, `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]` - existing AOT annotations
8. Look at GenericOperationSource.cs since it likely uses reflection
9. Look at ArmOperation*.cs files
10. Look at the Extensions/ directory
11. Look for any converter classes (files matching *Converter*)
12. Search for `Expression.` and `Delegate` creation patterns
13. Look at the Common/ directory structure and key model files
14. Look at Resources/ directory structure
15. Look at ManagementGroup/ directory

Report ALL findings with file paths and relevant code snippets. Be thorough - list every file and line that would need attention for AOT compatibility.

toolCalls.args:
{"name": "explore-aot-issues", "prompt": "Explore the .NET project in /tmp/sv-d57cc78fdb1c493495c4ad025e6cb367/before/ to identify AOT (Ahead-of-Time compilation) compatibility issues. This is an Azure.ResourceManager SDK project.

Look at all the key files and patterns that would typically cause AOT issues:

1. Look at the .csproj file for current AOT settings
2. Search for reflection usage patterns: `typeof`, `Type.GetType`, `Activator.CreateInstance`, `MakeGenericType`, `GetMethod`, `GetProperty`, `Invoke(`, `Assembly.Load`
3. Search for JSON serialization patterns - look for any `JsonSerializer`, `JsonSerializerOptions`, `JsonConverter`, custom converters
4. Look for `IUtf8JsonSerializable` and serialization infrastructure
5. Search for `dynamic` keyword usage
6. Look at the Shared/ directory for serialization infrastructure 
7. Search for `[UnconditionalSuppressMessage]`, `[DynamicallyAccessedMembers]`, `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]` - existing AOT annotations
8. Look at GenericOperationSource.cs since it likely uses reflection
9. Look at ArmOperation*.cs files
10. Look at the Extensions/ directory
11. Look for any converter classes (files matching *Converter*)
12. Search for `Expression.` and `Delegate` creation patterns
13. Look at the Common/ directory structure and key model files
14. Look at Resources/ directory structure
15. Look at ManagementGroup/ directory

Report ALL findings with file paths and relevant code snippets. Be thorough - list every file and line that would need attention for AOT compatibility.", "agent_type": "explore", "description": "Explore AOT compatibility issues", "mode": "background"}

data.agentDescription: Fast codebase exploration and answering questions. Uses code intelligence, {{grepToolName}}, {{globToolName}}, view, {{shellToolName}} tools in a separate context window to search files and understand code structure. Safe to call in parallel.

toolArgs.name: explore-aot-issues
toolArgs.prompt:
Explore the .NET project in /tmp/sv-d57cc78fdb1c493495c4ad025e6cb367/before/ to identify AOT (Ahead-of-Time compilation) compatibility issues. This is an Azure.ResourceManager SDK project.

Look at all the key files and patterns that would typically cause AOT issues:

1. Look at the .csproj file for current AOT settings
2. Search for reflection usage patterns: `typeof`, `Type.GetType`, `Activator.CreateInstance`, `MakeGenericType`, `GetMethod`, `GetProperty`, `Invoke(`, `Assembly.Load`
3. Search for JSON serialization patterns - look for any `JsonSerializer`, `JsonSerializerOptions`, `JsonConverter`, custom converters
4. Look for `IUtf8JsonSerializable` and serialization infrastructure
5. Search for `dynamic` keyword usage
6. Look at the Shared/ directory for serialization infrastructure 
7. Search for `[UnconditionalSuppressMessage]`, `[DynamicallyAccessedMembers]`, `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]` - existing AOT annotations
8. Look at GenericOperationSource.cs since it likely uses reflection
9. Look at ArmOperation*.cs files
10. Look at the Extensions/ directory
11. Look for any converter classes (files matching *Converter*)
12. Search for `Expression.` and `Delegate` creation patterns
13. Look at the Common/ directory structure and key model files
14. Look at Resources/ directory structure
15. Look at ManagementGroup/ directory

Report ALL findings with file paths and relevant code snippets. Be thorough - list every file and line that would need attention for AOT compatibility.

1. Look at the .csproj file for current AOT settings
2. Search for reflection usage patterns: `typeof`, `Type.GetType`, `Activator.CreateInstance`, `MakeGenericType`, `GetMethod`, `GetProperty`, `Invoke(`, `Assembly.Load`
3. Search for JSON serialization patterns - look for any `JsonSerializer`, `JsonSerializerOptions`, `JsonConverter`, custom converters
4. Look for `IUtf8JsonSerializable` and serialization infrastructure
5. Search for `dynamic` keyword usage
6. Look at the Shared/ directory for serialization infrastructure 
7. Search for `[UnconditionalSuppressMessage]`, `[DynamicallyAccessedMembers]`, `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]` - existing AOT annotations
8. Look at GenericOperationSource.cs since it likely uses reflection
9. Look at ArmOperation*.cs files
10. Look at the Extensions/ directory

result.detailedContent:
Prompt to explore agent (explore-aot-issues-3):
Explore the .NET project in /tmp/sv-d57cc78fdb1c493495c4ad025e6cb367/before/ to identify AOT (Ahead-of-Time compilation) compatibility issues. This is an Azure.ResourceManager SDK project.

Look at all the key files and patterns that would typically cause AOT issues:

1. Look at the .csproj file for current AOT settings
2. Search for reflection usage patterns: `typeof`, `Type.GetType`, `Activator.CreateInstance`, `MakeGenericType`, `GetMethod`, `GetProperty`, `Invoke(`, `Assembly.Load`
3. Search for JSON serialization patterns - look for any `JsonSerializer`, `JsonSerializerOptions`, `JsonConverter`, custom converters
4. Look for `IUtf8JsonSerializable` and serialization infrastructure
5. Search for `dynamic` keyword usage
6. Look at the Shared/ directory for serialization infrastructure 
7. Search for `[UnconditionalSuppressMessage]`, `[DynamicallyAccessedMembers]`, `[RequiresUnreferencedCode]` - existing AOT annotations
8. Look at GenericOperationSource.cs since it likely uses reflection
9. Look at ArmOperation*.cs files
10. Look at the Extensions/ directory
11. Look for any converter classes (files matching *Converter*)
12. Search for `Expression.` and `Delegate` creation patterns
13. Look at the Common/ directory structure and key model files
14. Look at Resources/ directory structure
15. Look at ManagementGroup/ directory

Report ALL findings with file paths and relevant code snippets. Be thorough - list every file and line that would need attention for AOT compatibility.

data.content: I'll systematically explore this .NET project for AOT compatibility issues. Let me start by examining the directory structure and then search for all the problematic patterns.
data.reasoningText:
The user wants me to explore a .NET project for AOT (Ahead-of-Time) compatibility issues. They've given me a comprehensive list of things to check. Let me break down the approach:

1. First, I should view the directory structure to understand the project layout
2. Then check the .csproj file
3. Search for all the specific patterns mentioned
4. Look at key directories and files

Since I can call multiple tools in parallel, I should start by:
1. Viewing the before directory structure
2. Finding the .csproj file
3. Doing multiple grep searches for the different patterns

Let me start with exploring the directory and finding key files, then do multiple parallel searches.
